sentences.text: Yes.
So these two men step out.
They are put in solitary confinement.
And within a week or so, a gallows has to be put up.
And one of the men has to volunteer to become the executioner, and they hang them for sending postcards.
No, no.
This is before we came.
So the regime was very much oriented, to start with, to make sure that people are afraid.
And it's very solitary, you know.
When-- I remember my brother, Leo, in Prague.
And he was a daring sort of man.
He would do anything.
And in Theresienstadt, he was careful, because he knew.
He had seen it.
I'll tell you just briefly how courageous Leo was.
He went on this AK-2 transport, with Edelstein, to Theresienstadt.
You know, presumably they were all volunteers.
That's why the Gestapo said, you will be able to protect your families.
In order to say goodbye to me and to his mother and to-- to my brother, he came from Prague to Valasske Mezirici.
Now, nowadays it's a four-hour train journey.
In those days, it was a whole day.
He came from Prague, not wearing the star, in 1941, without papers, all the way to Valasske Mezirici.
Came to see us, took me for a holiday to Prague.
Again, the same journey.
Again, not wearing stars.
Took me all around Prague.
I wanted to go to a theater.
And he said, no, we can't go to a theater, because there's a control at the box office.
They-- they want to look at your papers.
So we can go to the zoo, because the zoo was out of town.
But anything like the library or museums, we couldn't go, because that's where the Gestapo had their people who looked at papers.
So he knew where to go and where not to go.
He then takes me back to-- to Valasske Mezirici.
That's another day's journey.
And he takes Milos also for a week to Prague.
And he brings him back.
And that was my eldest brother, Leo.
Now, a man like that goes into Theresienstadt, and the Germans tell him to do something, he'll do whatever he thinks, you know, to beat the system.
But when two of his pals get hanged, he becomes a different man.
He began-- begins to calculate that this is no laughing matter here, you know?
We're not just-- we're not just interned.
How did that change in Leo affect you?
Well, we used to talk.
And Leo said, one of these days we're going to be somewhere outside, and I'll give you a tap, and I'll tell you to run.
When I tell you to run, you run.
Don't think about mother, and don't think about Milos.
When I tell you to run, you run.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK?
So this was how Leo saw it.
But he was going to be the person who would do it.
He would organize the circumstances.
Are you saying he was-- that was a plan to help you escape?
Yes.
And he, in fact, says, you go to the Seildels-- they lived two doors away from me, you know, in Vallaske Mezirici-- and they'll hide you.
Reason that we couldn't hide with the Seidels whilst we were being interned was because everybody knew that the Seidels were friendly with us.
So if we didn't turn up, first place the Gestapo would look would be the Seidels.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The eldest daughter of the Seidels is still alive, my age.
And when I saw her for the first time in 1990, there's this old woman comes to me.
I mean, I'm an old man, but there's this old woman comes to me, and says, Micha, Micha, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
Where have you been all my life?
[LAUGHS] So, I was supposed to run to the Seidels.
So, I knew that there was an underground type of activity.
And I knew that there was something going on.
But I was a kid, and what I wasn't told, I was not supposed to know.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] In the summer of 1943, we'd been there nearly a year, my sister-in-law, [?
Traute, ?] was working in a factory that was making bindings for the German army.
And because of that, she got extra rations.
Now, how did we get other extra rations?
Because Milos got a job in a kitchen.
So Milos would steal food from the kitchen.
And I would be the means of carrying it to [?
Traute, ?] who would somehow get it cooked.
And the best meal that we kids had, in the [INAUDIBLE], was buchty [SPEAKING CZECH].
I'm saying it in Czech, because we always laugh when we talk about this.
There was a sort of cake, which was made out of-- it was a roll, but it was soft dough.
It was a cakey type of thing.
Bit of sugar in it.
It was sweet.
And on top of it was a sauce, which was made out of coffee and margarine.
And the margarine wasn't dissolved in it, but somehow-- it wasn't emulsified.
I mean, they didn't have mixers and things-- the little bits of margarine would float in this sauce like yellow dots.
It was actually quite pretty.
You're making a face.
[LAUGHS] Not only was it pretty, but it tasted good.
It almost tasted as good as chocolate.
And, of course, we were starved for sweet and sugar.
So once a week we had buchty [SPEAKING CZECH].
That was a treat.
So my brother, Milos, was making this buchty, and he said, you come to this and this window at the kitchen.
There was a special kitchen where the cooking was done for children, because there were four or five of these buildings, huge things.
I mean, big organization.
My own school, which is now, by the way, the Museum of Theresienstadt, the same building, L-417 in the-- in the counting of Theresienstadt.
Each-- each house had a number according to the grid reference.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So this kitchen was in L-318, which was one block away.
Near the other fence, actually.
This-- you know the road that went through which divided north and south Theresienstadt, or Terezin, in Czech.
So he said, I'm going to be making these cakes.
And every other one or so, I'll throw out of the window.
And you'll stand outside the window and catch them.
[LAUGHS]

Now, I never realized this, but when somebody throws a tennis ball at you, to catch a tennis ball, you actually-- your reflex action follows the hand that's throwing, and you move whilst the hand is throwing.

It's tremendously difficult-- I don't know if you have ever tried it, but if you want a bit of fun, try a bit of dough, like bread dough, you know, just a-- like a piece of cake, but it's just a piece of dough, and throw it out of your first floor window at your children, grownups, or whatever.
Good sportsman or bad, they won't catch them.
Particularly if it's dough, you see, because it squashes as you dive for it.
Because you don't see the hand that's throwing it, and you have to watch the window.
And suddenly this thing appears, and you get-- you can't, in fact, gauge the direction.
It's the most funny thing.
Anyway, I got the hang of it after about three or four.
But you sort of get handicapped, because you put pieces of dough inside your shirt.
And after you have about six or seven of these bits of dough, the whole thing is weighing you down, you see?
And you're trying to jump this way and that way to catch them, because he doesn't always throw them the same dir-- direction.
So anyway, some of them got dirty.
But I picked-- I mean, it was precious stuff.
Picked it all up, brought it to my-- to [?
Traute. ?]
And we didn't have ovens.
She had a way of cooking, but not a way of baking.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But she sort of cooked it on top of the stove-- the stove, like [INAUDIBLE], you see.
And they became rock hard.
We kids could eat it.
It was delicious.
But my mother couldn't eat it, because of her teeth.
So she always took the food.
I'll eat it later.
And we all knew that whatever she put into her hand bag, she'd distribute around.
Once I went with Eve to San Francisco.
Obviously now, after the war.
And we visited a man called-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what's his name?
Karel.
He was in [INAUDIBLE] with me.
And he's not one of the Birkenau boys, because he was a bigger boy.
And we all went into his jacuzzi.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when we were in the jacuzzi on the balcony in San Salito, above San Francisco, you know, on the other side of the bridge, he says to me and Eve, you must excuse my little girls that they are looking at you strangely, because you are actually the first people outside the family who are with us in the jacuzzi.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We weren't even naked.
They were-- they always went into the jacuzzi naked.
But this time, we all had swim trunks.
So, he-- so I said, jokingly I said, well, Karel, what is the [SPEAKING CZECH]?
[SPEAKING CZECH].
What is-- you know, this is quite an honor that we're the first to be in the family jacuzzi.
Why are we deserving of such an honor?
So he says, oh, you are deserving because your mother used to feed my mother in Theresienstadt.
So I said to Eve, you see?
I was telling you these stories.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And I was sort of growing away from my mother, because she was looking after other people.
She wasn't looking after me.
The person who was looking after me was Leo and Milos.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that was stealing.
Summer of 1943, there was a big row in the whole of the ghetto.
Eichmann was coming to visit.
And there was the opening of this factory where-- I mean, the factory was already going, but he sort of, presumably, came to open the factory where [?
Traute ?] was working.
And there was a row between him and Edelstein.
We didn't know what it was.

But a few weeks later, the whole of the ghetto has to march out to an appel out in the valley between Bohusovice and Theresienstadt, which in Czech is called Bohusovicka kotlina. 'Kotlina' means valley.
It's actually a very flat valley.
The crematorium was in the Bohusovicka kotlina.
By the way, 'crematorium,' you mustn't run away with the idea of gassing or anything.
It was just people who died of natural causes.
Lots of people died in Theresienstadt of natural causes, because of the privations-- cold and, you know, old people.
If you got ill, you died.
Children died of various contagious diseases.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But-- so these bodies were disposed of in the-- in this crematorium.
Anyway, we were all going out to this county.
No, no.
It was once.
Once only, because we were never counted.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I could-- we were standing, all the young people together.
The Germans counted us once.
They counted us a second time.
We stood a whole day and into the night. 100,000 people.
It's quite a-- can you-- can you think of 100,000 people being counted, standing in so many-- you know, everyone stood with the people that he lived with, but in rows of five, counting.
And for some reason, the German couldn't-- the Germans couldn't come to the number that they required.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And between one counting and the other counting, there would be some sort of reckoning that went on.
It would be a few hours.
Leo comes, you know, red bands, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] two-- two first aiders with a stretcher.
And he says to me, quick, duck under.
And the stretcher bearers are taking me into the city, through the gate.
And this was something to do with the counting.
So this is all registered in a book, you know.
He says, hospital number so-and-so.
We're going into the city.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am then taken out through another gate, a different gate, as a body.
And I'm pretending-- you know, for heaven's sakes, don't breathe.
And don't-- so I'm being taken out by stretcher bearers.
And Leo isn't there.
And I'm being taken out as a body through a different gate.
And then I'm told, when you get to the Bohusovicka kotlina, you know, join the appel.
Now, there was a game being played by these men to confuse the count.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Later, when we got counted in Auschwitz and we were in the family camp, Leo explained.
And he said, there were people missing.
We knew there were people missing.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
The reason that they counted us was that two people got caught in Prague who were supposed to be in Theresienstadt.
The Gestapo said, how come?
So Eichmann came and said to Edelstein, how come?
Edelstein was taken into jail, and a new Alteste was appointed called Murmelstein.
Murmelstein was a German Jew.
Edelstein was a Czech Jew.
Edelstein was a Zionist.
Murmelstein was a-- presumably a real collaborator.
Murmelstein, by the way, after the war, took protection in the Vatican.
He was a historian, and he died.
He never went out of the Vatican again, because the Jews would have killed him.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Or somebody might have killed him.
Or he-- at least he was afraid that he would be killed.
Well, he continued to furnish the transports, because it seems-- I mean, we don't know.
We were not privy to the discussions between Edelstein and the commandant of Theresienstadt.
But presumably Edelstein said, I'm not preparing the transports anymore.
I resign.
In fact, Ruth Bondy in her book on Edelstein says that he resigned.
And the Germans said, this is not a resigning position.
You are appointed, and you will do as you're told.
Now, that's a construction, because we-- we were not privy to it.
And there are no documents to prove that this is so.
We only know that subsequently, Edelstein-- well, we know what happened afterwards.
Edelstein was in clink, and apparently the rumor went, in Theresienstadt, that there were-- there was a discrepancy of about 30 people.
And no way-- you know, these machinations, which were being done with bodies that were being shunted in and out, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] those machinations didn't make up the numbers.
But it always made a different number, whereas in Auschwitz, the Germans were quite insistent that every day they had to come to the exact number.
There was no-- you counted the bodies, and you counted the people, and there was no discrepancy.
And you stood all night.
Sometimes you stood all through the night and the next day until you came to the right number, because then they knew there was one escape or whatever.
There were a few in Auschwitz.
Well, we came back into Theresienstadt.
The next thing was that the proteczia is changing, because Murmelstein is now Judenalteste.
So, all the Zionist proteczia is disappearing, and all the [NON-ENGLISH] people on the-- on the Jugent-- on the Judenaltesterat are in a transport in September.
And Milos, myself, and my mom are in the transport.
Leo isn't, although he's on the Zionist list.
He's a doctor, so he's staying in the hospital where he's employed.
And his wife is also protected-- she was not on the Zionist list-- because her father was a high court judge, a Czech high court judge.

Whole story goes, with her father-- because her father was Doctor Kemp, who was the judge on the [INAUDIBLE]. 1937, there was a Heinlein Party MP in the Czech parliament who was advocating that the Sudetenland be annexed by the Reich.
And a Czech MP said to this [INAUDIBLE], you're a traitor.
And [INAUDIBLE] took out a pistol, in the parliament, and shot this Czech MP.
There was a trial.
And who should be the judge-- the Jewish judge?
Dr.
Kemp.
So Dr.
Kemp-- by the time that Leo married [?
Traute, ?] Dr.
Kemp was nowhere to be seen, because when the Germans came to [INAUDIBLE], the first person they took was Dr.
Kemp.
And Dr.
Kemp was never seen again.
You mentioned just now that you were on a transport.
Where--
So we were on the transport in September.
East.
That's all we knew.
I was no longer in Theresienstadt when the Red Cross visit took place.
That took place later.
[PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] I left December, 1943.
And the Red Cross visit was sometime in '44.
This [INTERPOSING VOICES], you know, making Theresienstadt lovely, that all happened before the Red Cross visit.
And it all happened in 1944.
No.
No.
It happened-- I was already in Auschwitz.
I can tell you about it, but it's only from reading.
Yes.
We were allowed, officially, postcards through the Red Cross.
And I think we sent ones from Theresienstadt.
And we sent it to Switzerland to an uncle-- incidentally, that was one other thing, that we were going to join Father through this address in Switzerland, which was a man called Avraham [?
Heizelbach, ?] who was the brother of my uncle.
My mother's sister, Perla, married Zev [INAUDIBLE], who was [?
Heizelbach. ?] They lived in Tel Aviv.
I have cousins called [INAUDIBLE] now in Tel Aviv.
And the whole story about Perla and how she died and how it influenced my mother to be anti-Zionist, and my mother's attitude to the war, it's all tied up with the [INAUDIBLE].
I can't go into it, because it's too-- it's a book.
It's my mother's tragedy, which is a completely different story.
I was talking about us being in the transport.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were already on the ramp boarding the train, when, again, Leo appears with a-- with a wheelbarrow this time and a stretcher party.
And he's got a paper which says that this family has a contagious disease.
He doesn't say it's my family.
He says, this family has a contagious disease.
I have to take them-- I have here an order to take them straight to quarantine.
If they stay, the whole train will arrive dead.
What a prospect.
[LAUGHS] And Leo had that sort of-- he spoke German perfectly.
So he was allowed to take these three people.
And off we went, back into the ghetto.
You probably don't know, but when the transports were being got ready, each train was 2,500 people.
And when the Germans said 2,500, they meant 2,500.
If people died-- there was a barracks where everybody, 2,500 people, waited until the train came.
The train came now all ready.
The railway station was outside this barracks.
So all you had to do was go out of the gate and straight into the train.
That's how the Germans liked it.
I mean, it was really very well done.
If you-- if you see the tracks which are left there in Theresienstadt, and the barracks where we came out of the gate, you could see that even Theresienstadt wouldn't know what's happening.
And you were all inside the ghetto, you see?
So people die.
Whilst you were waiting for the train to come, people would die.
So there were people who were told, you come to this barracks, and you are in the transport.
And there are-- there were people who were told, you're not in the transport, but you're in the reserve.
And there was always 200 or 300 people in the reserve, so that whoever died or was taken off because they were ill, last-minute changes, the number that went was the exact number.
So for us three, some reserve people went.
And I don't know who they were.
But somebody else went instead of us.
That was Proteczia or initiative or-- you-- you-- save your life at the expense of others.
Absolutely none.
I mean, you know, I was being told what to do.
And Leo said something, and I was-- it's the same [INAUDIBLE].
My elder brothers were like second fathers to me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, I did think about freedom and the fact that I couldn't go more than 300 yards and there was a wall, and that Leo forbade me to go and steal potatoes outside the wall.
Incidentally, the two lads that I went with was called [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE] was his name.
I don't remember his first name.
We called him [INAUDIBLE].
They were very daring boys.
And inside the room where we were about 30 boys, we made a sort of kernel of six.
And I was in this together with [INAUDIBLE].
But he survived.
This fella, [INAUDIBLE], he walked a little bit effeminate.
He wasn't effeminate, but he sort of walked a little bit effeminate.
And he was terribly courageous.
And they went for potatoes sort of every week.
And I was itching, you know, to go with them.
I had been out with them once.
We had to climb out from-- over this fence, go past the kommandantur onto the walls, and then, with a rope, down into the potato store.
And, I mean, two pounds of potatoes, perhaps a kilo, you would-- you'd be able to carry, because we had to climb fences.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE] survived the war in Theresienstadt.
I don't know what Proteczia they had.
And they both went to Venezuela, and they got killed robbing a bank.
They got shot robbing a bank.
Now, somehow they kept on organizing.
